risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This describes a computer-generated randomization procedure with stratification factors, which is a proper random allocation method and therefore supports low risk of bias for sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20).
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were assigned by a computer-generated urn randomization program, stratified by age, sex, race, and cigarettes smoked per day.
context_clues: assigned participants to MT or FFS
based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This describes sequence generation and stratification, but the article does not state any allocation concealment method (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers). Therefore we cannot tell whether the recruiter could foresee the next assignment, so the risk is unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation only: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." It does not describe concealment of the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All participants received twice weekly group sessions (eight total) ... delivered by instructors experienced in MT ... or certified in FFS respectively ... FFS was chosen as an active ‘standard treatment’ comparison condition" and "Participants were neither encouraged nor discouraged from using nicotine replacement in either group" Comment: Participants and treatment personnel were not blinded; the trial instead used an active behavioral comparison with matched session structure, but no placebo/sham or masking is described. Because the main outcomes included subjective smoking outcomes such as self-reported smoking, one-week point prevalence abstinence, average cigarettes smoked/day, and nicotine replacement use, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20). All participants received twice weekly group sessions (eight total) that were manualized and delivered by instructors experienced in MT ... or certified in FFS respectively ... FFS was chosen as an active ‘standard treatment’ comparison condition ... For example, both MT and FFS had a quit date at the end of week two (session four), were matched for length (1.5h/session) and delivered on the same days of the week (Monday and Thursday). In addition, home practice materials were matched in a number of ways ... Each participant received a meditation practice CD.”
_evidence.blinding_method: active ‘standard treatment’ comparison condition; matched sessions (same length, same days, matched home practice materials/CDs); no placebo/sham described

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB)... Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement" Comment: The outcome assessor for the main smoking outcomes was a research assistant not involved in treatment delivery, but there is no statement that this assessor was blinded to allocation. More importantly, the key outcomes include patient-reported smoking/abstinence, which are vulnerable to bias if participants knew their assignment. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded self-reported outcomes makes this domain high risk, even though CO verification is objective.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB) (Sobell and Sobell, 1992). Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement of ≤ 10 parts per million at each of the twice-weekly treatment and at follow-up visits.

outcomes.label: self-reported smoking / cigarettes smoked per day

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Sixteen (eight in each group) did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment." Comment: The reported post-randomization attrition on the analysed smoking outcomes was 8/41 vs 8/46 (total loss about 18.2%), balanced across arms with no differential loss, and the authors report an ITT mixed-effects approach with incomplete data handled by casewise deletion. The stated non-completion was not outcome-related and there is no evidence of imbalanced dropout due to lack of efficacy or adverse events, so attrition is unlikely to bias the result.

per_arm_flow.reasons: "eight in each group" did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment; "Participants who dropped out of treatment were contacted to provide in-person assessments at follow-up time points"

"eight in each group" did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment; "Participants who dropped out of treatment were contacted to provide in-person assessments at follow-up time points"

_evidence.missing_data_method: intent-to-treat mixed effect regression models; casewise deletion
_evidence.notes: Per-arm baseline randomization counts come from Table 1 / Results (MTS n=41, FFS n=46; total randomized 88, with 1 excluded from analysis in statistical models due to incarceration). Follow-up completion rates were reported at 6, 12, and 17 weeks among treatment-exposed individuals, but the attrition signal most directly documented is 8 per arm not exposed to treatment before baseline paperwork completion.
